title: TCP Flow Control
description: Mechanism that prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver using a sliding window.
fullExplanation: TCP flow control uses a sliding window to match the sender's transmission rate to the receiver's processing capacity. The receiver advertises its available buffer space (receive window) in every ACK. The sender may not have more than one window's worth of unacknowledged data in flight. If the receiver's buffer fills up, it advertises a window of 0 — the sender stops and waits for a window update. The window size field is 16 bits (max 65535 bytes), but the Window Scale option (RFC 1323) extends this to 1GB.

slidingWindow.description: The sender maintains a window of bytes it can send without waiting for ACK

states.description: Left edge of window — can be freed from buffer
Receiver has buffer space — can send immediately
Beyond window — must wait for ACKs to advance window

keyFacts: Receive window (rwnd): advertised by receiver in every ACK — available buffer space
Window size field: 16 bits = max 65535 bytes without scaling
Window Scale option (RFC 1323): multiplier up to 2^14 — allows windows up to 1GB
Zero window: receiver buffer full — sender stops. Sender probes with 1-byte segments.
Window update: receiver sends ACK with larger window when buffer drains
Silly window syndrome: avoid sending tiny segments — Nagle algorithm batches small writes
Flow control ≠ congestion control: flow control is end-to-end, congestion control is network-wide
